title: Market Structures
description: Perfect competition, monopoly, oligopoly, and monopolistic competition with Ethiopian examples

questions.statement: In perfect competition, individual firms are price takers.
questions.payload: TRUE. No single firm is large enough to influence the market price.

questions.statement: A perfectly competitive firm faces a horizontal demand curve.
questions.payload: TRUE. The firm can sell any quantity at the market price, making demand perfectly elastic.

questions.statement: In perfect competition, marginal revenue equals average revenue equals price.
questions.payload: TRUE. MR = AR = P because the firm sells every unit at the same market price.

questions.statement: Perfectly competitive firms can earn abnormal profits in the long run.
questions.payload: FALSE. Free entry attracts new firms, driving profits down to normal profit only.

questions.statement: A monopolist faces a downward-sloping demand curve.
questions.payload: TRUE. As the sole supplier, the monopolist faces the entire market demand which slopes downward.

questions.statement: In monopoly, marginal revenue is always greater than price.
questions.payload: FALSE. MR is less than price because the monopolist must lower price to sell more units.

questions.statement: A monopolist maximises profit where MR = MC.
questions.payload: TRUE. All profit-maximising firms produce where MR = MC, including monopolists.

questions.statement: A monopolist produces at the point where P = MC.
questions.payload: FALSE. A monopolist produces where MR = MC, and since P > MR, price exceeds marginal cost.

questions.statement: Ethio Telecom before privatisation was an example of a monopoly.
questions.payload: TRUE. Ethio Telecom was a single seller with a unique product and complete entry barriers.

questions.statement: The teff market in Ethiopia is an example of perfect competition.
questions.payload: TRUE. Teff has many sellers, a homogeneous product, and free entry into the market.

questions.statement: Oligopoly is characterised by a large number of small firms.
questions.payload: FALSE. Oligopoly has a small number of large, dominant firms.

questions.statement: In an oligopoly, firms are mutually interdependent in their pricing decisions.
questions.payload: TRUE. Each firm must consider rivals' likely reactions before changing price or output.

questions.statement: Cement factories in Ethiopia are an example of an oligopoly.
questions.payload: TRUE. Cement has a few large producers with difficult entry and mutual interdependence.

questions.statement: Monopolistic competition is basically the same as monopoly.
questions.payload: FALSE. Monopolistic competition is much closer to perfect competition with many firms and free entry.

questions.statement: Product differentiation is a key feature of monopolistic competition.
questions.payload: TRUE. Firms sell similar but not identical products, giving them some price control.

questions.statement: Hair salons and restaurants in Ethiopia are examples of monopolistic competition.
questions.payload: TRUE. They have many sellers, differentiated services, and relatively free entry.

questions.statement: In monopolistic competition, there are high barriers to entry.
questions.payload: FALSE. Monopolistic competition has low barriers to entry, similar to perfect competition.

questions.statement: Monopolistically competitive firms can make abnormal profits in the long run.
questions.payload: FALSE. Low barriers to entry attract new firms, eroding profits to normal profit only.

questions.statement: In perfect competition, the firm's demand curve is the same as its marginal revenue curve.
questions.payload: TRUE. Because price is fixed, every additional unit sold adds exactly the market price to revenue.

questions.statement: A natural monopoly arises when one firm can supply the entire market at a lower cost than multiple competing firms.
questions.payload: TRUE. High fixed costs mean a single firm benefits from economies of scale that rivals cannot match.
